rubric_items.reasoning: The agent read the live ConfigMap, confirmed it was at the expected pre-state (rev9), then applied a scoped `kubectl patch --type merge` on only the `data.value` field, and re-read the object to confirm the value landed as `rev10` with the ConfigMap otherwise untouched (same name/namespace/uid, unchanged annotations). A merge-type patch to a ConfigMap's data field is a standard, durable Kubernetes operation — it persists in etcd and would read back identically on any future `get`/restart. Oracle also confirms pass. This is a genuine, root-cause-correct fix, not a symptom-level or lucky pass.

The sequence is exactly diagnose-then-act-then-verify: (1) `get configmap -o yaml` to check current state, (2) a one-line reasoning note confirming state was as expected before patching, (3) the patch itself, (4) a final `get` to confirm the new value. No detours, no backtracking, no redundant calls — four turns total, each with clear purpose.

Used `kubectl patch --type merge` scoped to only the `data.value` key — the least-invasive way to update a single field without touching the rest of the object or risking a full replace/apply that could clobber other data. Reads were plain `get -o yaml` calls, no broad listing, no destructive flags, no scope creep beyond the single target resource.

Exactly one mutation (the merge patch) against the single required field, bracketed by two read-only verification calls. No unrelated resources touched, no unnecessary namespace-wide operations, no extra churn — this is the minimal possible action set to satisfy the task.
